speaker: interviewer
text: --on the 23rd of May, 1995, in Sydney Australia. Maurice, before you go on from Austria, could you tell me what happened to your brother, Henry, and your sister, Andzia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- my brother Henry survived the war. But, unfortunately, he died. But I'll tell you the truth. I don't like to talk much about my brother, because my brother really wasn't too much help for us. I must say this. And I'd like to speak as little as possible, because I'd be happier with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't too much help to us during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my sister? You mean

speaker: interviewer
text: Andzia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Andzia? Well, unfortunately, [SIGHS] from what I've heard, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my sister was taken on a ship, not by herself, with lots of people that were on Christian papers from Plaszow, from this murder Goeth. And they were thrown into the sea alive. And that's what I heard about my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: People who were in Plaszow after the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all I know about  my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I really do remember now, when I'm talking to you, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the-- one of my cousins, his name was [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he was in Plaszow. I didn't mention this before. I forgot all about it. And he used to see my sister in Plaszow. And he used to come to me to-- to my camp in [INAUDIBLE], where I was and gave me the message. And I used to pass on to my sister, who was on [? Christian ?] [? paper ?] to Celina. And his wife, when I came to Skarzysko, she was in Skarzysko, too, which I didn't know. From Plaszow, they put extra people into Skarzysko. And she was looking for me in Skarzysko. She heard that I was there. And she was so nice to me, like I would be her son. And she used to look after me. Not that she could do much, but whenever she could, she really used to cheer me up and things. And she is the one, too, who told me that' what happened to my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she didn't survive the war either.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I heard from a few people the same thing, so it must be true unfortunately. That's how she lost-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] So you went to Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I went skiing. And I told my sister that I think that I want stay in Poland. Because, permanently, I wouldn't like to stay in Poland. Because that wasn't a place where I would want to be after going through so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and to the army I didn't want to go. Because two years I would have to join the army. So I managed to do something. And while I was staying in Poland, I changed my dates. And I changed things, everything. And it was working till I went.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then I received other papers. Make the story short, I would have to join the army anyhow. So I decided to go. My sister was worrying very much, because all she had is me and [INAUDIBLE]. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I said, look, I have to go. And I decided to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to make sure that everything would be all right, they didn't let me go skiing, but they managed to get someone for money who would take me through the border. So I met them in Bielsko. And we went [INAUDIBLE]. We went, and we went into Bratislava. But on the way, before I had to get the train, the fellow who take me-- to make the story short-- he disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't have anybody. So I just got off the train. I had money with me, but I had has it hidden. I couldn't give it to someone who would ask me for money or something. I didn't have any documents with me, because if I would be caught on the border, I could say I got lost. Otherwise, I would be sent to Siberia then, for sure, for 10 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went on the train. And the train was going when the conductor came to me. I told him, look, I haven't got a ticket. But he was the kind of a fellow, I wasn't the first one, and he knew with whom he was dealing. He said, what have you got? And, you know, the Czech people are very nice people. And he wouldn't harm me. He said, have you got money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I haven't got any money. But I've got a watch. I've got a golden ring. I can give it to you. He said, all right. I give him my watch. And I give him my golden ring. I had a signet. And I said, if that-- that's all right. He said, it's all right. But I won't give you a ticket now. I'll give you a ticket before Bratislava. Because before Bratislava, the ticket won't be much. But I was afraid that someone might be coming to check the tickets. But he said, don't worry. I'll give you a ticket before you get off. And this is what happened. But in Bratislava-- no, that was and halfway to Bratislava, yes, we were waiting on a train. A different train, it supposed to be. That's when he gave me the ticket for the other-- that's right. That's when he gave me the ticket for the other train. And we would have to wait there for an hour for the other train. And I just sat down there and just sitting. And a fellow comes to me, a Czech. And he said to me something-- no, sorry, that's not how it was. I recognized him, that he could be Jewish. And in Poland, if you wanted to know if someone is Jewish, not to ask directly if you're Jewish, you said, are you [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: [HEBREW] .

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a Hebrew word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he didn't know what I was talking to. So I kept away. I said, sorry. And I kept away. But he realized that there's something.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to me. And he said, are you Jewish? I said, yes. He took me out. He said, be very, very careful. Because there were many accidents here. And if the Russians will get you, you'll finish up in Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, here's money. Here's my address, in Bratislava. As soon as you come to Bratislava-- because he asked   me if I have money, and I told him I got, but I can't get the money out. He says, here's Czech money. As soon as you come to Bratislava, you come to my place. You get a taxi in Bratislava, and you come. My wife is at home. And this is what I did. As soon as I arrived in Bratislava, I took a taxi. And I had the address to his wife. And she asked me to go to sleep. I slept there during the day. And then he came back. The next morning, they told me that there is a Bricha here. It's an organization. And people are going from there to Austria if I would like to go. I said, yes. I would love to go to Austria, because I know Austria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- I don't know how they managed to do it, but they bribed the Russians. Because the train went right through to Austria. And the Americans took us out. And I arrived to Austria in a place-- no, sorry, first, I went to Vienna. I'm sorry, I went to Vienna. And I met a relation. I was in Vienna for a few days, maybe a week-- I had the most beautiful time in Vienna-- to start with. And from Vienna, I went to Steyr. And in Steyr, I was there for a year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a very good time in Steyr. I used to do some business in Steyr.

speaker: interviewer
text: You worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used to do some business. It was mainly black market business. And I was very happy there. But I found out that is-- my sister wanted-- I was in touch with my sister. She was going to go, as far as I knew, or to America or to Australia. So to be on the safe side, I was registered to America, and I was registered to Australia. But then I found out that definitely my sister's going-- she's got papers for Australia. But she is in Paris. So when the time came for me to go to Australia-- not really to go to Australia, but to register myself at the consulate, I wasn't still sure if they were going to take me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in to the consulate. And it didn't take long. He questioned me and so and so. And then he wished me good luck. And he said, you are-- whenever the transport will go next, we'll notify you, and you will go to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thanked him very much. And I notified my sister that I'm going to go. I don't know when, but I'm going to go to Australia. And months after, the papers came. And I went by train to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Italy? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And in Italy, we were in a camp. What was the city called? It'll come back to me now. And we went there for quite a little while in this camp till they had a ship. And the ship was called It was a virgin ship. It was converted from a war ship to a passenger ship. But I had to do a two years contract in Australia. I to sign in Austria--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes

speaker: interviewee
text: --that I will do a contract for two years. And wherever they send me, I will go, which I was very, very happy to do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know much about Australia before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know nothing about Australia. All I was expecting here is to see black people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's all I knew about Australia. They only thing I knew-- I never heard the name Australia. But in Austria, I knew more. But I was interested already to find out. They told me there are white people, but there are people, too. So I expected to see black people in Australia. And when I arrived here in Australia, I, for the first time, I could have done exactly what the Pope did. I could have kissed the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the happiest person in my life. I was so happy. They took us to a place called Bonegilla. And first thing, I couldn't see the black people. I was only seeing the white people. I was so excited. I just-- not that it worried me. But I just couldn't believe that it's Australia. I've never heard in my life. When I was in school, I'd never heard anything about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not even kangaroos?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew Honolulu was the end of the world. That's what we knew. But we didn't know anything about Australia. So I was really so happy here. And I can honestly say I'm the happiest person in the world since I've come to Australia. I've met the most beautiful people. And I couldn't find myself in a better country. I worked very hard in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I worked very hard. I-- but I was very, very happy with it. I picked fruit. I had to do a three years contract. I had a letter, which I was dying to find for my book, sent by the government to thank me for fulfilling so nicely the job without any problems. I was picking fruit in Shepparton. I was working in New [INAUDIBLE]. I was in Albury. I was-- I was-- wherever they sent me, I went with pride. And I was so happy. And then, when my sister came to Sydney, I managed to ask my boss if I could go and see my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: He let me off for a week to go and see my sister. I came Melbourne to see my sister. And when I came back after the week, you wouldn't believe-- even the pay packet was there for me, though I wasn't working. So how couldn't you like Australia? Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, I enjoyed every bit, till today. And I always tell this my children, that there's nothing in the whole world like Australia. And at least I survived all of this. At least I came to the best country in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, I-- really, I met my wife in Poland. But she was only a child. I wouldn't even dream about it, because it's through relations, really. And I taught her, even, Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- because for the lessons that she needed--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came from the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come from the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Krakow, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I never dreamed of anything. But, you see, my sister and her uncle were friends in Poland. Because my sister's late brother-- husband was related to her uncle. And this is how. But I would never dream of her being my wife. And then, I left for Australia. And after a while, they came to Australia. And she was lovely girl. And then she went to high school here. And the last year of high school, we somehow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Got together.

speaker: interviewee
text: --got together. And a year after high school, we got married. And I couldn't find a better wife than I've got. Yes. And we are happy. And our children are happy. We are very, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two children, a boy and a girl. My son is a doctor now. And the girl finished university as well. And they married the most beautiful children. They are-- Nicki is a psychologist. And my son-in-law's an architect. And they are very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they've got the most beautiful-- and I have the most-- the children. And I have got the most beautiful five grandchildren. And what does could you wish for? What else could you wish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of work did you do after you finished your contract?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I finished my work after-- well, I was still under contract, matter of fact. And a lady in [INAUDIBLE], Mrs. Curtis-- well, I was living with my sister then. And this lady's husband passed away. And she asked me if it would be possible for me-- she had a lady's wear business. This is how I got into the lady's wear. And she asked me if I could work for her. Because her husband passed away, and she had two little children. I said, I don't know if I can, but if you'd like to go to the-- here to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] welfare and ask them if they agree to it, then it's all right with me. But without the government, I can't do anything. Because I have got a three year contract. And it's  still a long time to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she went. And because of her husband passed away and she two little children, they agreed that I can come. No, first-- sorry. When-- I asked for a permit to be transferred to Sydney, because my sister was in Sydney. So they let me go to Sydney. And I worked for a place, [INAUDIBLE] and [? Williams ?] in [INAUDIBLE], a toy factory. Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, she asked me, Mrs. Curtis asked me, if I can work for her. So they agreed to it. And I went to work for her. And I was staying there until I started on my own something, for five years till I got married. And this is how I-- I started my own business.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did manufacturing a bit on my own. Because I did a bit of designing here, of course. And I started on my own. I was doing manufacturing on my own. And then, I bought the business that I had. And I was quite successful. I was working quite hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: But nothing to be sorry for me, because it was very enjoyable. And I was quite successful. in it. I wasn't-- didn't become a-- such rich person. But I'm happy, whatever I have. Because I know what a piece of bread means.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm very, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I ask you what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yup.

speaker: interviewer
text: --happened to your religious beliefs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I be honest to you. I don't think, if I do-- I come from parents that they were religious. They were not fanatics, as I mentioned. They were very religious people. They were very nice Jewish people. But I-- I do go to shul. I go twice a year, maybe three or four times a year sometimes. Because of the kids, that's how I used to go. Because to my understanding, the kids should have their assigned religion as I had and I grew up with.

speaker: interviewer
text: But for what I've seen in my life, what I went through, what I've seen what they did with-- with little children, [SOBBING] with innocent little babies, what I see with my own eyes, how can I believe it? I don't say I don't. But how can I believe it? How can you take me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't pray. I don't say anything. But I would never, never in my life reject my Jewish religion, never. I was born a Jew. And I will die a Jew. I am not fanatic. And I hope my children will feel the same way as I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I-- I went through too much in my life, what I've seen, to believe that there's something. Because how can God-- sorry to say-- see such little ones? And it's not only then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I've been in Australia for 40-odd years. I see the same thing happening. It might be not exactly the way I see it. But the people are dying, which they shouldn't. Take Rwanda, take Bosnia, take all those countries-- unnecessary. And I still see those things through. Believe me, when I see it on television, I have to switch it off. Because I can't watch it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel any bitterness towards the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't feel bitterness, towards everyone, no. I don't. I try to avoid all those things. I try to be happy. And I try to forget. And after tonight, believe me, I won't discuss this with anyone anymore. I know that everyone should know. I'm sure this will still get people that will bring in the conversation. But I'm happy, really, that I did what I'm doing today for the whole world. And I've got one wish in my life. [SOBBING] And I hope that those things never happen again, and that no one experience what I did in my life. And this is my wish only for the whole world. That's what I can say. I've got nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Maurice.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know it hasn't been easy for you. And it's been--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I'm happy to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very, very happy to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I feel privileged to have done it with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much, too. It was not easy for you, three of you, here, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder if we could meet your wife and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, certainly. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And other members-- any other members of the family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father-in-law--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your father-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Maybe, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maurice, could you introduce other members of your family, your wife and your father-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, this is my lovely wife, Sally, that I met after the war. And we are happily married for 42 years. This is my father-in-law, which been living with us since the beginning of the-- since we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And god bless him, I hope he lives with us for another few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is 95.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Sally was interviewed this morning, weren't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was interviewed this morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So-- and the rest of your family's not here. But we'll see them on the photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You will see them on the photos. Unfortunately, they couldn't come, because they're at school. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the children, it might be too much of a hassle.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sally, how have you found living with someone so close to you, who's been through so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, I think it's-- we understand each other much better than if I would be married to someone who came from a different background. Because he's lived through so much. And I lived through-- probably not as much as what he did. I can understand him. And he understands. So if we have our-- our little sad moments, we know-- we know--

speaker: interviewee
text: What life is all about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mmm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what about your father-in-law? How has he fit into--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he fitted in very--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fits in.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that well. Because he must have also be through--

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he fitted in very well. And luckily, yeah, he doesn't look back on those sorrow times. If he talks to us, it's always the good times that he had with my mother and as-- where we were children. And he always tells me how my mother used to do things. And I'm not  doing the things--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you should do.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the same way. Unfortunately, she didn't live long enough to teach me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your children? How have they responded to your experiences? Have you spoken to them about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the-- your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandchildren, they might know a little bit. They know that we suffered during the war. But they don't know yet. They haven't read the book or anything. But my son and my daughter, they-- they know about it. And when my son had read the book, I'd never seen anybody go through what he did when he finished the book. It's just unbelievable. You know, he's a young boy and he goes through--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the first time that he knew about your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He knew before that. But we didn't talk much. But he knew that I was in concentrations camps. But he didn't know what I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: The detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they'll be watching the video. And your grandchildren one day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --will see the video. Is there anything you'd like to say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's what I-- to the grandchildren, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: The grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope they grow up healthy and they don't experience what we did. This is the main thing for me say to them. I hope they will never, never need  to experience-- not only them, but in all the world, no one. That's what I would say. Because this is just-- I can't believe myself that I went through it,  that's it's possible to go through such things. I can't believe it, myself. If not that I went through it, I-- I wouldn't believe if someone would come along and tell me a story like this. I don't know that a human being can go through all those things. It's impossible to believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the only message I've got for my grandchildren are that they be healthy and grow up to be a good citizen for Australia, at least that's what I can give to Australia for what they give me. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for the whole world, I hope they-- that when they see this in the world wherever they are, I hope that people believe that those things did happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right. Thank you, Maurice. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell him it's finished, because he can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just say who this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they're a group of children. They are neighbors from [INAUDIBLE]. And there is my sister, Marisha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this was taken in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute. OK. This one here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is-- this is Selena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Genia and Andzia. And this is just a neighbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: A neighbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And that's also taken in 1939, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This here?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Andzia. That's the one that--

speaker: interviewer
text: The one who died on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Now, this one, I am there, in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you there?

speaker: interviewer
text: 11? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is Genia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is Marisha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the neighbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: The neighbor, right. In 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Genia--

speaker: interviewer
text: When she was 15?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and Marisha.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she was 16 in 1941. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is the one when I survived-- after surviving Mauthausen--- about two weeks after surviving Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you there?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say your hair was just growing back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: --after it was shaved. And now this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Italy, where we went to Santa Maria.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's the soldier who was our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] commandant. And there, I am where it's pointing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's you with the arrow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, where the arrow is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And this was in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before you went to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I went to Palestine, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, Maurice, who are these two?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a photo taken in Australia with my beautiful wife, Sally. And we've been married now for 42 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was taken in 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son here-- on my side here, is my son and my daughter-in-law. They married here in Australia. And on the other side is my daughter and my son-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who's this, Maurice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In this photo here, they-- they are my two lovely grandchildren. One is Samantha and the other one is little Ashley.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whose daughters are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my daughter's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter's children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On this photo here, they're my three beautiful children again. And one is Daniel, Jamie, and Ben.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whose children are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are my son's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lovely. So that's a lovely legacy you've left behind, or you're going to leave behind--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: --out of all the trauma.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
